title: Aion Operations — Production Deployment, Observability, and Runtime Completion
intention: Make Aion deployable. Close the gap between 'tests pass' and 'production-ready'. When this cluster is done, aion-server is a production binary that a platform team can deploy behind a load balancer, point Prometheus at, wire into their alerting stack, and trust with real workloads.
problem: Non-resident signal delivery is unfinished — signals to suspended workflows are rejected instead of queued for delivery on resume.
No graceful shutdown or drain — rolling deploys lose in-flight work.
No structured observability — no metrics, no traces, no health probes. Operators cannot distinguish a healthy idle server from a crashed one.
No runtime configuration — store URLs, listen addresses, and thread counts require code changes to modify.
No authentication boundary — any client that can reach the port can operate in any namespace.

decisions.title: Non-resident signal delivery completes the AT-006 contract
decisions.chosen: Engine signal path gains residency-aware routing: Resident records and delivers, NonResident records and defers via existing SignalResumeHandoff, Terminal returns typed error. On workflow resume (Suspended -> Resident transition), engine invokes deliver_deferred() to flush queued signals FIFO.
rejected: Dropping signals to non-resident workflows silently
Polling-based signal delivery on timer rather than event-driven resume trigger
decisions.rationale: Temporal guarantees signal delivery regardless of workflow state. Dropping or polling violates the durability contract. The SignalResumeHandoff struct is already built and tested — only the wiring is missing.

decisions.title: Graceful shutdown with configurable activity drain
decisions.chosen: On SIGTERM: stop accepting new work, signal workers to drain, await in-flight activities up to configurable timeout, then shutdown engine. Clean drain exits 0, timeout exits 1.
rejected: Immediate shutdown killing all in-flight work
Indefinite wait for all activities (no timeout)
decisions.rationale: Rolling deploys must not lose work. But indefinite waits turn a deploy into a hang. A configurable timeout with retryable-failure surface for timed-out tasks gives operators control.

decisions.title: Prometheus metrics and Kubernetes-style health probes
decisions.chosen: Prometheus exposition format on /metrics endpoint. Liveness probe (/health/live) checks scheduler responsiveness. Readiness probe (/health/ready) checks store reachability and optionally worker connectivity.
decisions.rationale: Prometheus is the de facto standard for infrastructure metrics. Direct exposition avoids requiring an OTel collector in every deployment. Health probes match Kubernetes pod lifecycle expectations.

decisions.title: TOML configuration with environment override and CLI precedence
decisions.chosen: Single aion.toml file, AION_ prefixed env vars override, CLI flags override both. Precedence: CLI > env > file > defaults.
decisions.rationale: TOML is readable, comments-friendly, and Rust-native (serde support). Env vars enable container deployments where config files are awkward. CLI flags enable one-off overrides in scripts.

decisions.title: Feature-gated JWT authentication with namespace-scoped claims
decisions.chosen: When auth.enabled=true, Bearer tokens validated against JWKS endpoint. Token claims include namespace field scoping all operations. Feature-gated behind cfg(feature='auth') to keep base binary lean.
decisions.rationale: JWT/JWKS is the industry standard for service-to-service auth with structured claims. Feature gating keeps the dependency tree clean for embedded and testing use cases where auth is irrelevant.

goals: A signal sent to a non-resident workflow is recorded and delivered when the workflow resumes, matching Temporal signal delivery semantics.
A rolling deploy (SIGTERM then drain then restart) loses zero in-flight work.
A Prometheus scrape of /metrics returns all listed metrics with correct labels and values.
/health/live and /health/ready return correct status codes within 100ms.
aion-server starts from a TOML config file with zero code changes.
With auth enabled, a request without a valid token is rejected with 401.
All of the above pass under cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings.
non_goals: Horizontal scaling or clustering — single-writer-per-workflow is the architecture; multi-node is a future concern.
Custom metrics backends — Prometheus exposition is the interface; operators pipe to their own stack.
OAuth2 provider implementation — the server validates tokens, not issues them.
Log aggregation — structured JSON to stdout; operators handle shipping.
Rate limiting — deferred to a future cluster.

constraints.text: No execution logic in the server. Signal wiring (AO-001) is an engine change. The server never makes durability decisions.
No arbitrary defaults for operator-configurable values. Thread counts, timeouts, drain durations come from config.
Feature-gated auth. The auth module is behind cfg(feature='auth'). Base binary compiles without auth dependencies.
No new dependencies in the engine crate. Observability and config live in aion-server.
Structured output only. No println, no unstructured log lines. Everything through tracing with structured fields.
